Good feedback is specific, actionable, and focused on the work rather than the person. 'The sight line from the entry reveals the boss room too early, removing the surprise. Consider adding a bend in the corridor.' Bad feedback: 'the level needs work.'

The final level does not need to be flawless. It needs to demonstrate that the designer can apply the course's principles, collect and interpret playtest data, and iterate based on evidence. A flawed level with thoughtful analysis is better than a polished level with no process documentation.

The 'what went wrong' section is the most valuable part of any post-mortem. Admitting that the pacing sagged, the puzzle was obscure, or the combat was unbalanced demonstrates the reflective practice that defines professional designers.

quizzes.topic: Level Design Principles, Pacing, and Player Guiding

questions.question: A playtest reveals that 80% of players in your level run straight past an optional reward cave because they never notice its entrance. The cave entrance is on the left wall of a corridor where the critical path leads right and forward. Using level design principles from this course, describe three specific modifications you would make to draw players toward the cave without placing a UI marker on it.
questions.answer: Modification 1: Place a weenie visible through the cave entrance -- a glowing object, unique color, or light source that contrasts with the corridor's palette, creating a visual attractor that catches the player's peripheral vision. Modification 2: Bend the corridor so the player's forward path briefly faces the cave entrance before curving right. This forces the cave into the player's central vision for 1-2 seconds of travel, creating awareness without forcing a detour. Modification 3: Add a directional audio cue (running water, wind, distinct ambient sound) emanating from the cave that contrasts with the corridor's audio environment, alerting audio-attentive players. These three modifications use layered redundancy (visual, geometric, and audio channels) to reach players with different perceptual tendencies.

rubric.key: Proposes a visual attractor (weenie/lighting/contrast) at the entrance
Proposes a geometry or audio modification that increases awareness
Explains why the modifications work using course principles

questions.question: Draw or describe an intensity curve for a 10-minute level segment that contains: a puzzle room, a combat arena, a breathing room, and a boss encounter. Label each section on the curve and explain why you ordered them the way you did.
questions.answer: The curve follows a sawtooth pattern: (1) Puzzle room at medium intensity (2-4 minutes) -- cognitively engaging but not physically threatening. (2) Combat arena at high intensity (2-3 minutes) -- the puzzle solution opens the arena, and the difficulty spike rewards the mental effort with action. (3) Breathing room at low intensity (1-2 minutes) -- a safe space with a save point, ammo refill, and environmental storytelling. (4) Boss encounter at peak intensity (3-4 minutes) -- the climax, drawing on skills from both the puzzle and combat sections. The ordering creates two sawtooth cycles: puzzle builds to combat (first release), breathing room builds to boss (second and larger release). Starting with the puzzle eases the player in cognitively before demanding reflexive action.

rubric.key: Correct sawtooth shape with ascending intensity peaks
Each section labeled with appropriate intensity level and duration
Justifies ordering with pacing principles

questions.question: Explain the teaching triad and give a specific example of how a platforming mechanic (wall jumping) would be introduced, tested, and challenged across three consecutive rooms.
questions.answer: The teaching triad introduces a mechanic safely, tests it with moderate risk, and challenges it under pressure. Room 1 (Introduce): A short gap with walls on both sides. Falling results in landing on a safe platform just below, so failure costs 2 seconds of re-climbing. The player can experiment with wall jumping timing without punishment. A visual prompt (scuff marks on the walls) hints at the mechanic. Room 2 (Test): A taller gap requiring 2-3 consecutive wall jumps. Falling drops the player to a lower path that loops back to the start of the gap, costing 15-20 seconds. The stakes are higher but not punishing. Room 3 (Challenge): Wall jumping over a hazard (lava, spikes) while avoiding moving obstacles (swinging pendulums) between the walls. The timing window is tight, failure sends the player back to a checkpoint, and the challenge combines wall jumping with obstacle reading from earlier in the level.

rubric.key: Correctly defines the three stages of the teaching triad
Room 1 introduces safely with low failure cost
Room 2 tests with moderate risk and increased complexity
Room 3 challenges under pressure with combined mechanics

quizzes.topic: Environmental Puzzles and Combat Encounter Design

questions.question: You are designing a combat arena for a third-person shooter. The encounter should force the player to use both cover-based tactics and flanking maneuvers. Sketch or describe the arena layout including: entry points, cover positions, enemy placement, and at least one flanking route. Explain how the geometry creates the intended tactical behavior.
questions.answer: The arena is a combat bowl: a central depression 20m in diameter, ringed by a 2m-high elevated perimeter. The player enters from a corridor at the south, stepping down into the bowl. Cover cluster: 4-5 waist-high barriers in the center of the bowl provide initial cover. Enemy placement: 3 ranged enemies on the north perimeter (elevation advantage), 2 melee rushers spawning from east and west ground-level doors after 10 seconds. Flanking route: a narrow tunnel beneath the east perimeter connects the south entry to the east side, allowing the player to emerge behind the east melee spawn. The geometry forces flanking because the central cover protects from the north snipers but not from the east/west rushers. The player must use the flanking tunnel to clear the rushers before pushing up the north ramp to engage the snipers.

rubric.key: Clear arena layout with entry, cover, and enemy positions described
Flanking route that creates tactical decision-making
Explains how geometry forces the intended combat behavior

questions.question: Explain the principle of progressive disclosure in puzzle design. Why is it superior to presenting the player with all puzzle mechanics simultaneously? Give an example of a three-room puzzle sequence that progressively discloses mechanics.
questions.answer: Progressive disclosure introduces one mechanic at a time, allowing the player to master each in isolation before combining them. Presenting all mechanics simultaneously overwhelms the player with too many variables, making it impossible to isolate which mechanic solves which part of the puzzle. Example: Room 1 teaches light redirection -- the player aims a mirror to redirect a beam of light onto a sensor, opening a door. Simple and clear. Room 2 teaches color filtering -- the player passes white light through colored filters to produce red, green, or blue light, matching the sensor's required color. Room 3 combines both -- the player must redirect a light beam through mirrors AND pass it through the correct color filter sequence to produce the target color on a sensor at the end of a multi-mirror path. Each room adds exactly one new element to the player's toolkit.

rubric.key: Correctly defines progressive disclosure and its cognitive benefit
Explains why simultaneous presentation is inferior
Provides a concrete three-room example with clear progression

questions.question: What is the difference between difficulty through geometry and difficulty through numbers? Give one example of each for the same combat encounter, and explain why geometry-based difficulty is generally considered better level design.
questions.answer: Difficulty through numbers: increasing enemy health from 100 to 200 HP and adding 3 more enemies to the same arena. The player does the same thing but it takes longer and requires more resources. Difficulty through geometry: removing two cover positions, adding a sniper perch at elevation behind the player's entry point, and making the flanking route narrower. The player must change their tactics -- they cannot camp behind cover because the rear sniper threatens them, and the reduced cover forces more aggressive movement. Geometry-based difficulty is better because it forces the player to develop new strategies and use different skills, creating a learning experience. Number-based difficulty just inflates the time and resource cost of the same strategy, which feels tedious rather than challenging. Players remember the encounter where they had to change tactics, not the one where enemies had more health.

rubric.key: Clear example of number-based difficulty increase
Clear example of geometry-based difficulty increase
Explains why geometry-based is superior (forces tactical adaptation)

quizzes.topic: Open World Design, Grayboxing, and Lighting for Gameplay

questions.question: Compare a hub-and-spoke level structure with a fully open-world structure. For each, describe one strength and one weakness related to pacing control. Then name a game that uses each structure effectively.
questions.answer: Hub-and-spoke: Strength -- each spoke can be internally paced like a linear level, giving the designer control over difficulty curves and narrative beats within each self-contained area. Weakness -- the player must return to the hub between spokes, which can feel repetitive and interrupts momentum. Example: Dark Souls uses bonfires as hubs connecting to self-contained areas with carefully paced encounters. Open world: Strength -- the player has complete freedom to explore at their own pace, creating a sense of discovery and personal narrative. Weakness -- the designer cannot control the order or pacing of content consumption, so intensity curves are unpredictable and narrative coherence suffers. Example: Breath of the Wild lets players tackle any region in any order, with systemic interactions creating emergent pacing that compensates for the lack of authored pacing control.

rubric.key: Correct strength and weakness for hub-and-spoke with pacing justification
Correct strength and weakness for open world with pacing justification
Appropriate game examples for each structure

questions.question: Explain why character metrics (jump height, sprint speed, cover height) must be established before grayboxing begins. Describe what happens to a level's gameplay if the graybox is built without reference to these metrics.
questions.answer: Character metrics define the spatial constraints that all level geometry must respect. Jump height determines gap width and ledge height. Sprint speed determines corridor length and timing puzzle windows. Cover height determines barrier dimensions. If grayboxing begins without these metrics, the designer will build spaces at arbitrary scale. Gaps may be unjumpable or trivially jumpable. Cover may be too tall (player cannot shoot over it) or too short (provides no protection). Corridors may be so long that traversal is boring or so short that encounters blend together. When character metrics are established later, the entire graybox must be rebuilt because the proportions are wrong. Metric-first grayboxing saves time by ensuring every dimension is functional from the start.

rubric.key: Explains the role of character metrics in spatial design
Describes specific problems from metric-less grayboxing
Connects to time savings from metric-first approach

questions.question: Describe how a level designer would use lighting to communicate that a player is transitioning from a safe area to a dangerous area, without any text, UI elements, or enemy visibility. List at least three specific lighting changes.
questions.answer: (1) Color temperature shift: transition from warm light (3500K, orange/amber) in the safe area to cool light (7000K, blue/white) approaching the dangerous area. The gradual shift triggers unconscious unease because humans associate warm light with shelter and cool light with exposure. (2) Contrast increase: the safe area has soft, even lighting with low shadow contrast. The dangerous area has harsh, directional lighting with deep shadows, creating visual tension and hiding potential threats in the dark areas. (3) Light source change: the safe area uses visible, domestic light sources (torches, lamps, fires) that feel controlled and human. The dangerous area uses sourceless ambient light or eerie sources (bioluminescent fungi, flickering industrial lights) that feel unnatural and unpredictable. Together, these three changes create a clear emotional gradient that communicates danger through visceral perception rather than explicit information.

rubric.key: Color temperature shift from warm to cool
Contrast or shadow change that increases tension
Third distinct lighting change with emotional justification

quizzes.topic: Procedural Generation, Multiplayer Design, and Accessibility

questions.question: Compare the BSP dungeon generation algorithm with Wave Function Collapse. For each, describe how it generates a layout, then explain one scenario where each algorithm is the better choice.
questions.answer: BSP recursively subdivides a bounding rectangle into leaf nodes that become rooms, then connects sibling nodes with corridors, guaranteeing full connectivity by construction. WFC places tiles one at a time from a set of hand-authored examples, collapsing each tile's possibilities based on adjacency constraints propagated from already-placed neighbors. BSP is better for traditional dungeon-crawlers (roguelikes) where rectangular rooms with corridor connections are the expected style and guaranteed connectivity is critical. WFC is better for organic or stylistically specific environments (town layouts, cave systems, overworld regions) where the output must match the aesthetic of hand-crafted examples and tile-level visual coherence matters more than geometric regularity.

rubric.key: Correct description of BSP algorithm
Correct description of WFC algorithm
Appropriate scenario for each with valid reasoning

questions.question: You are designing a multiplayer capture-the-flag map. Explain three specific design principles you must follow to ensure the map is balanced and fun for both teams. For each principle, describe what happens if it is violated.
questions.answer: Principle 1: Symmetrical or equivalent spawn-to-flag distances. Both teams must reach the center of the map in approximately the same time. Violation: one team reaches the flag first every round, creating an unfair first-engagement advantage that compounds across the match. Principle 2: Multiple routes between bases. At least 3 paths (main route, flanking route, high-risk shortcut) prevent defensive lockdowns. Violation: with only one path, a single defender can completely block flag carriers, making the game a stalemate with no tactical variety. Principle 3: Contestable power positions. High ground, sniper perches, and chokepoints must be approachable from multiple directions. Violation: an uncontestable position lets one team lock down the map with a single player, removing strategic decision-making from the game.

rubric.key: Three distinct, valid design principles for CTF balance
Clear explanation of what happens when each is violated
Principles are specific to multiplayer design, not generic

questions.question: Explain the principle of sensory redundancy in accessible level design. Give a specific example of a level design element that violates this principle and describe how you would fix it.
questions.answer: Sensory redundancy means that critical gameplay information must be communicated through at least two sensory channels (visual + audio, or visual + haptic, etc.) so that players with impairments in one sense can still receive the information. Violation example: a stealth level where the player must avoid searchlights that sweep the area, but the searchlights make no sound. A deaf player receives no warning when a searchlight is about to sweep their position. Fix: add an audio cue (a mechanical humming that gets louder as the light approaches) AND a controller vibration that intensifies with searchlight proximity. Now the information is available through vision (seeing the light), hearing (hearing the hum), and touch (feeling the vibration). Any two of these three channels provide sufficient warning.

rubric.key: Correctly defines sensory redundancy
Provides a specific violation example
Fix adds at least two additional sensory channels

questions.question: You run a playtest with 10 players and observe: Room 1 (100% reach), Room 2 (100%), Room 3 (50%), Room 4 (50%), Boss Room (30%). Identify the most critical design problem, describe what data you would collect to diagnose it, and propose a specific hypothesis for the cause.
questions.answer: The critical problem is the 50% drop between Room 2 and Room 3 -- half the players cannot progress past that point. Next data: death heat map in Room 2/3 transition, time-spent data for the 50% who did not reach Room 3, and think-aloud recordings to determine if players are stuck (ability gate), dying (difficulty spike), or lost (navigation failure). Hypothesis: The transition from Room 2 to Room 3 contains an ability gate (a jump gap or puzzle mechanic) that was not adequately taught in Rooms 1-2. Players who happen to discover the mechanic progress while others do not. This is consistent with a binary 50% pass/fail drop rather than a gradual decline, suggesting a discrete barrier rather than accumulated attrition.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies Room 2-to-3 as the primary drop point
Specifies appropriate data to collect for diagnosis
Proposes a specific, testable hypothesis

questions.question: Explain why the one-change-per-iteration principle is important in level design iteration. Describe a scenario where violating this principle leads to a worse outcome than following it.
questions.answer: One-change-per-iteration ensures that the designer can attribute observed improvements (or regressions) to a specific change. When multiple changes are made simultaneously, it is impossible to determine which change caused the effect. Scenario: a designer observes that players get lost between Room 2 and Room 3 (50% drop). They simultaneously (a) add a guiding light to Room 2's exit, (b) widen the corridor, and (c) remove an enemy from the transition. The completion rate improves to 80%. But they do not know whether the light, the width, or the enemy removal caused the improvement. Later, when a different area has the same problem, they apply all three changes when perhaps only the lighting was needed, wasting development time on unnecessary modifications. Worse, if the next playtest shows a new problem caused by the widened corridor (players now skip content), they cannot undo just the corridor change without potentially losing the improvement from the lighting.

rubric.key: Explains the attribution problem from simultaneous changes
Provides a concrete scenario showing the negative outcome
Connects to long-term consequences for design decisions

questions.question: What are the three things a hiring manager looks for in the first 90 seconds of reviewing a level design portfolio? For each, describe one specific portfolio element that demonstrates it.
questions.answer: (1) Engine competence -- can this person build a playable level? Demonstrated by: a downloadable or video-embedded playable level in Unreal or Unity, not just concept art or paper designs. The portfolio must show implemented, functional game spaces. (2) Spatial design understanding -- do they think about player flow, pacing, and encounter design? Demonstrated by: an annotated top-down map with callouts for sight lines, pacing zones, guiding techniques, and encounter areas. This single image communicates spatial thinking more effectively than beauty screenshots. (3) Communication clarity -- can they explain their design decisions? Demonstrated by: a concise case study with design goals, process documentation (concept -> graybox -> playtest -> iteration), and a post-mortem that honestly assesses strengths and weaknesses. Clear writing signals a designer who can collaborate with a team.

rubric.key: Identifies engine competence with a specific demonstration element
Identifies spatial design understanding with a specific element
Identifies communication clarity with a specific element

major_assignments.title: Complete Level Design: From Concept to Playtested Graybox
major_assignments.description: Design, graybox, and playtest a complete single-player game level in Unreal Engine 5. The level must be 8-12 minutes of gameplay, include at least one puzzle sequence, one combat arena, and one exploration/reward area. You must conduct at least three formal playtesting sessions with different participants and iterate on the design based on observed data. Submit both the original and iterated graybox to demonstrate how playtesting changed your design.
objectives: Apply the full level design pipeline from concept document through grayboxing to playtested iteration
Demonstrate mastery of pacing, player flow, guiding techniques, and spatial composition
Use playtesting data to make evidence-based design decisions documented with before/after comparisons

Unreal Engine project with both original and iterated graybox versions saved as separate maps

Iteration log documenting each change with before/after data and rationale
Post-mortem reflection (2-3 pages) analyzing what changed, why, and what you would do differently

major_assignments.title: Multiplayer Map Design with Procedural Variation
major_assignments.description: Design and build a balanced multiplayer map for a team-based game mode (capture the flag, control point, or team deathmatch) in Unreal Engine 5. The map must demonstrate symmetrical or asymmetrical balance, managed sight lines, contestable power positions, and flow loops. Additionally, implement one procedural element: either a BSP-generated dungeon wing, a WFC-generated decorative layer, or procedurally placed pickup/spawn variations that create replay variety. Playtest the map with at least 4 players in two sessions.
objectives: Design a competitively balanced multiplayer map with intentional sight line and flow management
Implement procedural generation for replay variety while maintaining design quality
Balance spawn points, power positions, and objectives for fair competitive play
Collect and analyze multiplayer-specific playtest metrics (kill heatmaps, win rates by side)
deliverables: Unreal Engine project with playable multiplayer map supporting the chosen game mode

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all playtesting sessions and critiques. Missing a playtesting session without prior arrangement will result in a zero for the associated exercise, as playtesting requires participant presence.
course_policies.late_submissions: Design documents and written assignments lose 10% per day late. Graybox projects cannot be submitted late because they require in-class playtesting on specific dates. Arrange alternatives at least one week in advance.
course_policies.collaboration: Playtesting requires partnering with classmates, but all design work and grayboxing must be individual. You may not use another student's graybox layout as a starting point for your own work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Level designs must be original work. Recreating published game levels is acceptable only for analysis exercises, not for graded design submissions. All reference material and inspiration sources must be cited.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students must have Unreal Engine 5.3 installed with at least 50 GB of free disk space. A mouse with at least two buttons is required for viewport navigation. Lab machines are available for students whose hardware cannot run UE5.
